drills.scenario: Maya runs a Shopify pet brand selling premium dog supplements. She has 150+ SKUs and wants to launch her first Meta campaign. She's about to set up a regular link-post ad sending cold traffic straight to her full product catalog page so people can browse everything.
drills.question: What is the biggest problem with Maya's plan, and what should she do instead?
options: Sending cold traffic straight to a catalog with no rapport or desire messaging fails — she should pick her single top seller and run one of the two proven ecom funnels, messaging the audience's biggest frustration or ultimate desire
Catalog pages convert fine on cold traffic — her real problem is not having a lookalike audience built yet
She should send cold traffic to all 150 products but add a coupon code to boost conversions
The catalog is fine; she just needs a higher budget to give the algorithm enough data
drills.explanation: The module warns: 'Don't send cold traffic straight to a catalog or bare coupon' — running a regular link-post ad to all products fails without rapport, interest, or desire messaging. The 'Pick one best product, keep it simple to start' tactic says even with 150+ products, run one proven ecom funnel on your single top seller, messaging the audience's ultimate desire or biggest frustration. Retargeting and dynamic product ads come later as 'icing on the cake.'

drills.scenario: A real-estate coach created a lead magnet titled 'How to flip houses in five simple steps' — a 70-page eBook that fully teaches his method. He's thrilled: it generated thousands of cheap leads. But after weeks, almost none have bought his $2,000 8-module course, which covers the same material.
drills.question: Why is this funnel failing to produce buyers, per the module?
options: His ad targeting is too broad, so the leads aren't qualified
He violated the Incompleteness Principle — the lead magnet gave away the whole solution, so there's no reason to buy the paid course; the goal of a lead isn't a lead, it's a buyer
His tripwire is priced too high at $2,000 — tripwires should be under $20
He needs a webinar instead of an eBook because the course is high-ticket
drills.explanation: This is the module's headline pitfall. The offer must fulfill the hook's promise yet stay INCOMPLETE so the paid product delivers the bigger solution. Giving everything away ('five simple steps' fully taught) produced thousands of cheap leads but ZERO buyers because the paid course taught the same thing. The book's fix was a new, incomplete hook: '17 words you need to say to an investor to fund your next deal' → a 3-page checklist → far more purchases.

drills.scenario: Devon is a fitness coach selling a $997 program. He wants to use the book's approach of working backward to design a congruent hook-and-offer funnel before he builds anything. He has his notes on the market's desires and pain points in front of him.
drills.question: Put the 'Find a Solution / craft the hook & offer' steps in the correct order the module teaches.
options: Review your notes on the market's desires and pain points
Force-rank those pain points and desires
Separate wants from needs — they are not the same
Define the end-goal / core offer first
Devise the offer that fulfills the hook's promise but stays incomplete
Devise the hook that promises the solution
Confirm each step (hook → free offer → paid offer) is a logical, incomplete progression toward the core product
drills.explanation: The checklist starts with reviewing market desires/pain points (the logical starting point), then force-ranking them, then separating wants from needs. Then you 'Work Backward From the End Goal to the Hook': define the core offer/end goal first, then the offer, then the hook — and finally confirm each step is a logical, incomplete progression toward the core product. Defining the end goal before the offer and hook is the key sequencing insight.

drills.scenario: A local martial-arts gym wants its very first Meta campaign to bring in trial members. The owner is a complete beginner to Meta ads and keeps simple operations. He's deciding which of the Six Proven Facebook-Friendly Offers to launch with.
drills.question: Which offer best fits a local business launching its first campaign, and how should it be delivered?
options: Offer #6 Product Sales Page with an educational video — it's the most powerful ad type so it'll convert best
Offer #4 Webinar/Event — gyms should warm cold traffic with a 90-minute web class first
Offer #1 Coupon (e.g., '7 days free' or a '$50 off' offer) delivered via a simple image-based link-post ad to a coupon landing page with a thank-you page
Offer #3 Quiz/Survey funnel because branch logic will personalize the trial offer
drills.explanation: Offer #1 (Coupon/Sweepstakes) is the lowest-commitment offer and 'works wonders for local businesses' (e.g., 7 days free at a gym, BOGO, free consultation). Beginners should start with just ONE offer for momentum, and the Local/Retail funnel's recommended starting point is the simple image-based link-post ad ('keep it simple'). A thank-you page is required (on-platform coupons are no longer allowed) and helps filter lead quality. The webinar and educational sales-page video are higher-commitment, more advanced plays.

drills.scenario: A supplement brand runs a cold-traffic ad with a bold red headline: 'Is Your Metabolism Aging You?' Click-through is strong — lots of cheap clicks — but the landing page is a generic blue-themed homepage headlined 'Welcome to NutriCo — Quality You Can Trust,' and conversions are under 5%. The owner assumes the offer is weak.
drills.question: What is the most likely root cause the module would diagnose first?
options: The offer itself is bad and needs to be scrapped and replaced
Weak ad scent — the landing page's headline, message, and color scheme don't match the ad's hook, so congruency is broken; fix the page to match the ad before blaming the offer
The audience is too cold and needs a webinar before any landing page
The budget is too low for the algorithm to optimize conversions
drills.explanation: This is an ad-scent (ad-to-landing-page congruency) failure. The module stresses matching the ad copy, headline, and even color scheme to the hook: 'Weak ad scent kills conversions even with strong clicks; easy to overlook, easy to fix.' With a ~20% benchmark and under-10% signaling a real problem, the broken congruency (red hook ad → generic blue 'Welcome' page) is the first thing to check and fix — not the offer.

drills.scenario: A B2B consultant sells a $3,500 done-with-you program. She wants to acquire customers from cold Meta traffic. She's torn between (a) a short coupon ad to a discount page, (b) a direct long-form sales page, or (c) a webinar funnel — and she's heard 'registrations' are all that matter for webinars.
drills.question: Which approach fits this high-ticket cold-traffic situation, and what nuance must she remember?
options: A coupon offer to a discount landing page — discounts always work best for high-ticket
A webinar/live-event funnel (ideal for $500+ products because you get more time to warm cold traffic) — but registration is only half the battle: prospects must also show up AND consume the content
A direct long-form sales page straight from a cold ad — high-ticket buyers prefer to skip webinars
Whichever is cheapest to build; the offer type doesn't matter at this price point
drills.explanation: The module pegs the webinar/event offer as ideal for high-priced products ($500+ threshold) because you get more time with prospects to deliver value and warm cold traffic. But it explicitly warns 'Webinar registration is only half the battle' — even a free webinar is a big ask, and your best prospects value time as much as money, so they must register, show up, AND actually consume the content.

drills.scenario: A DTC skincare brand has been running purely demographic and interest-based ad sets for months across dozens of audiences. A consultant tells them their funnel is 'complicated, not sophisticated' and recommends the Bow Tie Formula. They want to understand what actually makes a funnel sophisticated.
drills.question: Which statements correctly describe a SOPHISTICATED funnel under the Bow Tie Formula? (Select all that apply.)
options: Most campaigns are driven by remarketing instead of static demographic/interest targets
A truly powerful funnel has many sequences (IF/THEN statements) — more touches mean more chances to build value and raise LTV
Piling on tons of demographic and interest ad sets across many audiences is the mark of sophistication
Delivering value repeatedly before making an offer (Value, Value, Value, Offer) builds goodwill so the funnel feels like a trusted friend's recommendation
Higher LTV from more touches lets you afford to spend more to acquire each lead
drills.explanation: Under the Bow Tie Formula, 'sophisticated' means most campaigns are driven by remarketing (not static demographic/interest targets), built from many sequences (IF/THEN statements) where more touches build value, raise LTV, and let you spend more per lead. 'Value, Value, Value, Offer' reinforces this goodwill-first approach. Option 3 is the explicit pitfall: piling on demographic/interest ad sets is 'complicated, not sophisticated' — the exact confusion the module warns against.

drills.scenario: A guitar instructor has a flagship 7-lesson 'Learn Rock Guitar' course. He wants a front-end offer to attract cold Meta traffic that naturally leads people toward buying the full course, without giving the whole thing away.
drills.question: Which front-end offer best applies the Splintering principle?
options: Give away the entire 7-lesson course free as a lead magnet so people experience the full value
Splinter off one popular, highly actionable chunk — e.g., '5 killer licks' from the most-popular riffs lesson — given away but kept incomplete so they want the full course
Sell the full course directly from a cold ad with a coupon to lower the barrier
Run a quiz asking which guitar brand they own, then email them the course link
drills.explanation: Splintering Your Offer means splintering off an easily consumable, highly actionable, highly valuable, popular chunk of your main product as the front-end offer for cold traffic — the book's exact example is a 7-lesson rock-guitar course → most-popular riffs lesson → '5 killer licks.' You give away your best stuff but keep it INCOMPLETE so they want more. Giving away the entire course (option 0) would repeat the house-flipping mistake and produce no buyers.
